title: Spotted dick
subtitle: Steamed suet pudding with currants and lemon.
excerpt: A classic British steamed suet sponge studded with dried currants, flavoured with lemon zest and served with custard. Steamed for two hours in a covered basin, the result is a dense, yielding, warmly spiced pudding. Serves four to six.
sourceNotes: Spotted dick. The name derives from 'spotted' (the currants) and likely 'dick' as a corruption of 'dough' or 'dough-cake', or possibly 'dich' (dialect for dough). The recipe appears in Victorian cookbooks throughout the 1800s. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) documents several suet pudding recipes of this type. It has been a staple of British institution cooking since the 19th century.

content.text: Steamed puddings need constant attention to the water level: the pan must never run dry. Keep it at a steady simmer, not a furious boil, and top up with boiling water from a kettle every 30 minutes. The two-hour steam is not negotiable; a shorter time produces an uncooked centre.

Mix {{self-raising-flour}} of flour, {{lard}} of grated or crumbled lard, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, {{currants}} of currants and the zest of {{lemon}} lemon together. Stir in {{whole-milk}} of milk to form a stiff dough. Spoon into a greased 1-litre pudding basin. Cover with a double layer of baking paper with a pleat in the centre, then foil, and tie securely with string. Set in a large saucepan and pour in boiling water to come halfway up the basin. Cover the pan and steam over medium-low heat for 2 hours, topping up with boiling water every 30 minutes.
Rest the pudding for 5 minutes before removing the cover. Run a knife around the edge and invert onto a plate. Serve immediately with poured custard.

items.cause: Insufficient steaming time, or water boiled away.
items.fix: Two hours at a steady simmer is non-negotiable. Test with a skewer — it should come out clean.

items.symptom: Pan ran dry and pudding is ruined
items.fix: Set a timer every 30 minutes to check. Only top up with boiling water, never cold, or the temperature drops and the steam stops.
Butter the basin generously, including the rim. A piece of baking paper on the base also helps.

content.text: Spotted dick is one of the most British of puddings: dense, warming, unflashy and completely satisfying on a cold evening. The name causes amusement on international menus but the dish is ancient and entirely serious. Currants, lemon and suet are a traditional combination in British baking, appearing in everything from mince pies to Chelsea buns.
